article_title: SPACs: The Downfall of the IPO
news_headline: OpenAI Leans Toward Waiting Until 2027 for IPO, NYT Says
post_text:
SpaceX priced its IPO at $135 per share on June 12, raised over $85 billion, and briefly hit a $2.77 trillion valuation. Within two weeks, the stock had fallen back to roughly $150, erasing most of the post-debut gains and Elon Musk's trillionaire status along with them. Now, according to the New York Times, OpenAI is leaning toward pushing its own IPO to 2027. CEO Sam Altman had been targeting a $1 trillion valuation for a late-2026 listing, but advisers are warning that retail enthusiasm for another mega-cap AI debut may not be there.

What the SpaceX episode exposed is something the IPO market has always known but rarely admits openly: the window between private hype and public pricing is narrow, and when it closes, it closes fast. OpenAI's last private valuation was between $730 billion and $852 billion. Getting to $1 trillion in a shaky market, right after the most-watched tech listing in history lost a quarter of its value in days, is a hard sell.

WBR's piece on SPACs and the evolving IPO landscape examined exactly this tension, how public market conditions, retail investor appetite, and valuation expectations collide when a company tries to time its debut. The SpaceX-to-OpenAI chain of events is that argument, live. Read more in our article, "SPACs: The Downfall of the IPO."

#BusinessNews #IPO
final_body:
